([wallstreetzen.com](https://www.wallstreetzen.com/stocks/us/nyse/ko/statistics?utm_source=openai)) Coca-Cola’s 2024 advertising expense and 2022/2024 net operating revenue come from its 2024 10-K. ([sec.gov](https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/21344/000002134425000011/ko-20241231.htm)) PepsiCo’s 2024 advertising/marketing spend and net revenue figures come from its 2024 10-K / annual report disclosures. ([investor.pepsico.com](https://investor.pepsico.com/docs/pepsico-5v9wci20/media/Files/investors/q4-2024-form-10k.pdf?utm_source=openai))

### Quick read

- **Share momentum:** **Coca-Cola is gaining / extending its lead** in U.S. CSDs. PepsiCo’s CSD share slipped from **24.7% to 23.8%** over 2022–2024, while Coca-Cola is reported around the mid-40s by 2024 and widened the gap in 2023.
- **Brand dollars:** **PepsiCo reports the larger 2024 marketing line** at **$5.9B** for advertising and other marketing activities, but on a strict advertising-expense basis it reported **$3.9B**, below Coca-Cola’s **$5.146B** advertising expense.
- **Revenue growth:** Coca-Cola grew net operating revenue faster over the period (**+9.4%**) than PepsiCo (**+6.3%**), though PepsiCo remains much larger in absolute revenue because it includes snacks/Frito-Lay and other foods, not just beverages.

results.question: Corporate Profile report of The Walt Disney Company
results.actual_answer:
# Corporate Profile Report: The Walt Disney Company

**Company:** The Walt Disney Company  
**Ticker:** NYSE: DIS  
**Headquarters:** Burbank, California, United States  
**Industry:** Diversified entertainment, media, sports, streaming, theme parks, resorts, cruise lines, and consumer products  
**Current CEO:** Josh D’Amaro  
**CFO:** Hugh Johnston  
**Board Chair:** James P. Gorman  
**Latest share price available:** **$108.66**; market capitalization approximately **$192.5 billion** as of the latest market data returned for May 7, 2026 UTC. 

---

## 1. Executive Summary

The Walt Disney Company is one of the world’s largest diversified entertainment and media enterprises. Disney operates across three core business segments: **Disney Entertainment**, **ESPN**, and **Disney Experiences**. Its competitive model is built around monetizing intellectual property — characters, franchises, stories, and brands — across theatrical releases, streaming, television, sports, theme parks, cruises, merchandise, games, and licensing. Disney describes itself as a “leading diversified international family entertainment and media enterprise.” ([thewaltdisneycompany.com](https://thewaltdisneycompany.com/about/))

As of fiscal 2026, Disney is navigating a major business transition: shifting entertainment and sports consumption from linear television toward direct-to-consumer streaming, while continuing to expand its high-margin Experiences business. In Q2 fiscal 2026, Disney reported **revenue of $25.2 billion**, up **7% year over year**, and **total segment operating income of $4.6 billion**, up **4%** year over year. ([s206.q4cdn.com](https://s206.q4cdn.com/979796730/files/doc_financials/2026/q2/q2-fy26-earnings.pdf))

---

## 2. Business Segments

### A. Disney Entertainment

Disney Entertainment includes the company’s global entertainment media and content businesses, including studios, television, streaming, and related content distribution. Key brands and assets include Disney, Pixar, Marvel, Lucasfilm, 20th Century Studios, Searchlight Pictures, ABC, FX, National Geographic, Disney+, Hulu, and Hotstar-related assets where applicable. ([thewaltdisneycompany.com](https://thewaltdisneycompany.com/about/))

**Q2 FY2026 performance:**

| Metric | Q2 FY2026 | YoY Change |
|---|---:|---:|
| Revenue | **$11.7 billion** | **+10%** |
| Segment operating income | **$1.3 billion** | **+6%** |

Disney reported that Entertainment subscription and affiliate revenue grew **14%** year over year in Q2 FY2026, while Entertainment advertising revenue grew nearly **5%**. Management noted that streaming revenue growth is now more than offsetting declining linear revenue in parts of the Entertainment business. ([s206.q4cdn.com](https://s206.q4cdn.com/979796730/files/doc_financials/2026/q2/q2-fy26-earnings.pdf))

### B. ESPN / Sports

The ESPN segment includes Disney’s sports media operations, including ESPN networks, ESPN+, and sports-related direct-to-consumer initiatives. ESPN remains a central asset for live sports distribution, advertising, and affiliate-fee economics. Disney stated that ESPN is the number one sports media brand in the U.S. and is strategically important as sports viewing migrates toward streaming. ([s206.q4cdn.com](https://s206.q4cdn.com/979796730/files/doc_financials/2026/q2/q2-fy26-earnings.pdf))

**Q2 FY2026 performance:**

| Metric | Q2 FY2026 | YoY Change |
|---|---:|---:|
| Revenue | **$4.6 billion** | **+2%** |
| Segment operating income | **$652 million** | **-5%** |

Sports operating income declined primarily because of higher rights fees and higher marketing costs. Disney also stated that ESPN subscription and affiliate revenue grew **6%** in the quarter, supported by direct-to-consumer revenue and the NFL transaction. ([s206.q4cdn.com](https://s206.q4cdn.com/979796730/files/doc_financials/2026/q2/q2-fy26-earnings.pdf))

### C. Disney Experiences

Disney Experiences includes theme parks, resorts, cruise lines, vacation clubs, consumer products, and related physical experiences. This segment is a key earnings engine for Disney because it converts the company’s intellectual property into immersive, high-value consumer experiences.

Key assets include Walt Disney World Resort, Disneyland Resort, Disneyland Paris, Hong Kong Disneyland Resort, Shanghai Disney Resort, Disney Cruise Line, Disney Vacation Club, Adventures by Disney, National Geographic Expeditions, and Aulani in Hawaii. ([stockanalysis.com](https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/dis/company/?utm_source=openai))

**Q2 FY2026 performance:**

| Metric | Q2 FY2026 | YoY Change |
|---|---:|---:|
| Revenue | **$9.5 billion** | **+7%** |
| Segment operating income | **$2.6 billion** | **+5%** |

Disney said Q2 FY2026 Experiences revenue and operating income were fiscal second-quarter records. Domestic parks per-capita spending increased **5%**, driven by admissions, food and beverage, and merchandise growth. ([s206.q4cdn.com](https://s206.q4cdn.com/979796730/files/doc_financials/2026/q2/q2-fy26-earnings.pdf))

---

## 3. Latest Financial Snapshot

### Q2 Fiscal 2026 Results

For the quarter ended **March 28, 2026**, Disney reported:

| Metric | Q2 FY2026 | Q2 FY2025 | YoY Change |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Revenue | **$25.168 billion** | $23.621 billion | **+7%** |
| Income before income taxes | **$3.367 billion** | $3.087 billion | **+9%** |
| Total segment operating income | **$4.603 billion** | $4.436 billion | **+4%** |
| Diluted EPS | **$1.27** | $1.81 | **-30%** |
| Adjusted EPS | **$1.57** | $1.45 | **+8%** |
| Cash provided by operations | **$6.914 billion** | $6.753 billion | **+2%** |
| Free cash flow | **$4.941 billion** | $4.891 billion | **+1%** |

EPS means **earnings per share**. Adjusted EPS excludes certain items and is a non-GAAP measure; Disney identifies income before income taxes and diluted EPS as the most comparable GAAP measures for segment operating income and adjusted EPS, respectively. ([s206.q4cdn.com](https://s206.q4cdn.com/979796730/files/doc_financials/2026/q2/q2-fy26-earnings.pdf))

### Six-Month FY2026 Results

For the six months ended **March 28, 2026**:

| Metric | Six Months FY2026 | Six Months FY2025 | YoY Change |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Revenue | **$51.149 billion** | $48.311 billion | **+6%** |
| Income before income taxes | **$7.060 billion** | $6.747 billion | **+5%** |
| Total segment operating income | **$9.203 billion** | $9.496 billion | **-3%** |
| Adjusted EPS | **$3.20** | $3.22 | **-1%** |
| Free cash flow | **$2.663 billion** | $5.630 billion | **-53%** |

The six-month picture shows revenue growth but weaker operating income and free cash flow versus the prior year, reflecting timing effects, investment, costs, and segment-level pressures. ([s206.q4cdn.com](https://s206.q4cdn.com/979796730/files/doc_financials/2026/q2/q2-fy26-earnings.pdf))

---

## 4. Fiscal 2025 Baseline

For fiscal 2025, Disney reported **revenue of $94.425 billion**, up **3%** from fiscal 2024, and **income before income taxes of $12.003 billion**, up **59%**. Total segment operating income increased to approximately **$17.6 billion** for the full year. ([investors.thewaltdisneycompany.com](https://investors.thewaltdisneycompany.com/news/news-details/2025/The-Walt-Disney-Company-Reports-Fourth-Quarter-and-Full-Year-Earnings-for-Fiscal-2025/default.aspx))

Disney’s fiscal 2025 segment highlights included:

- **Entertainment:** Full-year segment operating income increased **19%** to **$4.7 billion**.  
- **Experiences:** Full-year segment operating income reached a record **$10.0 billion**, up **$723 million** from the prior year.  
- **Sports:** Q4 operating income was relatively stable year over year, though the segment continues to face rights-cost pressure. ([investors.thewaltdisneycompany.com](https://investors.thewaltdisneycompany.com/news/news-details/2025/The-Walt-Disney-Company-Reports-Fourth-Quarter-and-Full-Year-Earnings-for-Fiscal-2025/default.aspx))

---

## 5. Strategy and Growth Drivers

Disney’s current strategy is centered on three pillars:

1. **Investing in intellectual property and creativity** that can generate long-term franchise value.  
2. **Reaching more consumers globally** through seamless digital and physical experiences.  
3. **Using advanced technologies** to improve storytelling, monetization, personalization, and returns. ([s206.q4cdn.com](https://s206.q4cdn.com/979796730/files/doc_financials/2026/q2/q2-fy26-earnings.pdf))

Key growth areas include:

### Streaming profitability

Disney reported that Entertainment SVOD revenue growth accelerated to **13%** in Q2 FY2026, compared with **11%** in Q1 FY2026. SVOD means **subscription video on demand**, including Disney+ and Hulu subscription services. Disney also reported its first double-digit Entertainment SVOD operating margin in Q2 and said it remains on track to deliver at least **10%** Entertainment SVOD operating margin for full-year fiscal 2026. ([s206.q4cdn.com](https://s206.q4cdn.com/979796730/files/doc_financials/2026/q2/q2-fy26-earnings.pdf))

### ESPN direct-to-consumer transition

Disney is positioning ESPN for a future in which live sports consumption increasingly shifts to streaming. The company said ESPN’s digital subscriber revenue in Q2 more than offset secular declines in the linear subscriber universe. ([s206.q4cdn.com](https://s206.q4cdn.com/979796730/files/doc_financials/2026/q2/q2-fy26-earnings.pdf))

### Experiences expansion

Disney continues to expand physical experiences through parks, cruise ships, and themed lands. In March 2026, Disney launched the **Disney Adventure** cruise ship and opened **World of Frozen** at Disneyland Paris. Disney said guest response has been positive and that Disney Adventure bookings have been very strong. ([s206.q4cdn.com](https://s206.q4cdn.com/979796730/files/doc_financials/2026/q2/q2-fy26-earnings.pdf))

### Franchise monetization

Disney’s model depends heavily on franchise flywheels. A successful film or series can support streaming engagement, theatrical revenue, consumer products, parks attractions, cruises, games, and licensing. Disney cited **Zootopia 2** as an example, noting that it generated **$1.9 billion** in global box office and helped the broader Zootopia franchise surpass **1 billion hours streamed** on Disney’s service. ([s206.q4cdn.com](https://s206.q4cdn.com/979796730/files/doc_financials/2026/q2/q2-fy26-earnings.pdf))

---

## 6. Leadership and Governance

Disney’s senior leadership includes:

| Role | Executive |
|---|---|
| Chief Executive Officer | **Josh D’Amaro** |
| President and Chief Creative Officer | **Dana Walden** |
| Chief Financial Officer | **Hugh Johnston** |
| Chairman, Disney Entertainment Studios | **Alan Bergman** |
| Chairman, Disney Experiences | **Thomas Mazloum** |
| Chairman, ESPN | **James Pitaro** |
| Executive Vice President, Investor Relations and Corporate Strategy | **Benjamin Swinburne** |

Disney’s board is chaired by **James P. Gorman**. Josh D’Amaro also joined the board in 2026. ([thewaltdisneycompany.com](https://thewaltdisneycompany.com/about/))

---

## 7. Shareholder Returns and Outlook

Disney’s latest Q2 FY2026 outlook included:

- Expected fiscal 2026 adjusted EPS growth of approximately **12%**, excluding the impact of the 53rd week.  
- Expected fiscal 2026 adjusted EPS growth of approximately **16%**, including the 53rd week.  
- Targeting at least **$8 billion** in share repurchases in fiscal 2026.  
- Expected Q3 FY2026 total segment operating income of approximately **$5.3 billion**.  
- Continued expectation for double-digit adjusted EPS growth in fiscal 2027, excluding the impact of the 53rd week. ([s206.q4cdn.com](https://s206.q4cdn.com/979796730/files/doc_financials/2026/q2/q2-fy26-earnings.pdf))

Earlier, Disney had declared a fiscal 2026 cash dividend of **$1.50 per share**, payable in two installments of **$0.75** each: one on January 15, 2026, and another scheduled for July 22, 2026, subject to the stated record dates. ([investors.thewaltdisneycompany.com](https://investors.thewaltdisneycompany.com/news/news-details/2025/The-Walt-Disney-Company-Reports-Fourth-Quarter-and-Full-Year-Earnings-for-Fiscal-2025/default.aspx))

---

## 8. Competitive Position

Disney’s major strengths include:

- Globally recognized brands and intellectual property.
- A diversified monetization model across media, streaming, sports, parks, cruises, merchandise, and licensing.
- Scale in streaming through Disney+ and Hulu.
- ESPN’s strong position in live sports.
- High-value Experiences assets, including parks and cruise lines.
- Ability to turn successful stories into multi-decade franchises.

Its key challenges include:

- Declining linear television economics.
- Rising sports rights costs.
- High content and technology investment needs.
- Consumer sensitivity in parks and experiences during periods of macroeconomic uncertainty.
- Execution risk in streaming profitability and ESPN’s direct-to-consumer transition.
- Dependence on creative success and franchise performance.

---

## 9. Investment-Style Assessment

Disney appears to be in a **transition-and-growth phase**. The company is no longer simply a legacy media and parks operator; it is attempting to reshape itself around streaming, sports direct-to-consumer distribution, and expanded experiential monetization. The Experiences segment remains the company’s most consistent profit engine, while Entertainment and ESPN are undergoing structural transitions from linear TV toward streaming.

The Q2 FY2026 results were broadly constructive: revenue grew 7%, adjusted EPS rose 8%, and segment operating income rose 4%. However, six-month free cash flow was down significantly year over year, and Sports operating income declined because of rights-cost pressure. For investors or analysts, the key watch items are streaming margin sustainability, ESPN’s direct-to-consumer execution, parks demand, capital spending, and the ability of Disney’s creative slate to generate franchise-level returns.

results.question: How many customers are unassigned to any collections pool in USMF as of March 2, 2026?
results.actual_answer:
**1,032 customers** in **USMF** are unassigned to any collections pool as of **March 2, 2026**.

results.question: Building a seniors acquisition plan. Compare UnitedHealth Group and CVS Health in Medicare Advantage for 2024: membership by top five states, year over year membership growth, and how 2024 and 2025 CMS rate updates influenced their guidance and medical loss ratios. Present the findings in one side-by-side table (companies as columns, metrics as rows) and include a brief summary. Report money amounts and percentages to two decimal places, using official US GAAP sources.
results.actual_answer:
**Assumptions / scope:** I treated “Medicare Advantage” as individual + group MA lives reported by the companies at **December 31, 2024**; for state-level membership I used **CMS Monthly Enrollment by Plan — December 2024**, aggregated by parent organization and state. Company financial metrics are from **official company GAAP filings / earnings releases**; note that **MCR/MBR (medical care ratio / medical benefit ratio)** is a company segment operating metric, not a GAAP line item, but it is reported in the companies’ official SEC/investor materials. State membership counts are CMS administrative enrollment data, not GAAP.

| Metric | **UnitedHealth Group / UnitedHealthcare** | **CVS Health / Aetna** |
|---|---:|---:|
| **2024 MA membership — company reported** | **7.85 million** Medicare Advantage people served at Dec. 31, 2024, versus **7.70 million** at Dec. 31, 2023. ([businesswire.com](https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20250116305758/en/UnitedHealth-Group-Reports-2024-Results?utm_source=openai)) | **4.45 million** Medicare Advantage members at Dec. 31, 2024, versus **3.46 million** at Dec. 31, 2023. ([fintel.io](https://fintel.io/doc/sec-cvs-health-corp-64803-10k-2025-february-12-20131-2904?utm_source=openai)) |
| **YoY MA membership growth** | **+0.15 million**, or **+1.95%** YoY. | **+0.99 million**, or **+28.53%** YoY. |
| **Top 5 states by 2024 MA membership — CMS Dec. 2024 enrollment file** | **Florida:** ~**1.06 million**; **Texas:** ~**0.86 million**; **California:** ~**0.75 million**; **New York:** ~**0.69 million**; **Ohio:** ~**0.45 million**. Source: CMS Monthly Enrollment by Plan, Dec. 2024, aggregated by parent organization/state. ([cms.gov](https://www.cms.gov/data-research/statistics-trends-and-reports/medicare-advantagepart-d-contract-and-enrollment-data/monthly-enrollment-plan/monthly-enrollment-plan-2024-12?utm_source=openai)) | **Florida:** ~**0.65 million**; **Texas:** ~**0.52 million**; **Pennsylvania:** ~**0.38 million**; **California:** ~**0.36 million**; **New York:** ~**0.29 million**. Source: CMS Monthly Enrollment by Plan, Dec. 2024, aggregated by parent organization/state. ([cms.gov](https://www.cms.gov/data-research/statistics-trends-and-reports/medicare-advantagepart-d-contract-and-enrollment-data/monthly-enrollment-plan/monthly-enrollment-plan-2024-12?utm_source=openai)) |
| **2024 rate-update backdrop** | CMS’s 2024 MA rate announcement began the three-year phase-in of the updated CMS-HCC risk-adjustment model and a technical adjustment to remove MA-related medical education costs from the fee-for-service benchmark baseline; CMS stated the **2024 effective growth rate was 2.28%**, after a **0.78%** reduction for 33.00% implementation of the medical-education technical update. ([cms.gov](https://www.cms.gov/newsroom/fact-sheets/fact-sheet-2024-medicare-advantage-and-part-d-rate-announcement)) | Same CMS backdrop; Aetna’s larger 2024 MA growth made the rate/star/utilization environment more visibly disruptive to its Health Care Benefits results. |
| **Effect on 2024 guidance / results** | UnitedHealth said the **2024 full-year medical care ratio was 85.50%**, up from **83.20%** in 2023, and attributed the increase partly to “CMS Medicare funding reductions,” member mix and Medicaid redeterminations. ([unitedhealthgroup.com](https://www.unitedhealthgroup.com/content/dam/UHG/PDF/investors/2024/2025-16-01-uhg-reports-fourth-quarter-results.pdf?utm_source=openai)) | CVS repeatedly revised 2024 guidance lower during the year; final 2024 results showed Health Care Benefits adjusted operating income of **$0.31 billion**, down from **$5.58 billion** in 2023, with management citing increased utilization and the unfavorable impact of 2024 MA Star Ratings. ([investors.cvshealth.com](https://investors.cvshealth.com/news/news-details/2025/CVS-HEALTH-CORPORATION-REPORTS-FOURTH-QUARTER-AND-FULL-YEAR-2024-RESULTS)) |
| **2024 MLR / MCR outcome** | **85.50% MCR**, up **2.30 percentage points** from **83.20%** in 2023. ([unitedhealthgroup.com](https://www.unitedhealthgroup.com/content/dam/UHG/PDF/investors/2024/2025-16-01-uhg-reports-fourth-quarter-results.pdf?utm_source=openai)) | **92.50% MBR**, up **6.30 percentage points** from **86.20%** in 2023. ([investors.cvshealth.com](https://investors.cvshealth.com/news/news-details/2025/CVS-HEALTH-CORPORATION-REPORTS-FOURTH-QUARTER-AND-FULL-YEAR-2024-RESULTS)) |
| **2025 CMS rate update** | CMS finalized an average **3.70%** MA payment increase for 2025, or **over $16.00 billion** versus 2024, but continued the 2024 CMS-HCC model phase-in at a **67.00% / 33.00%** blend and raised the medical-education technical-adjustment phase-in to **52.00%**. ([cms.gov](https://www.cms.gov/newsroom/fact-sheets/2025-medicare-advantage-and-part-d-rate-announcement)) | Same industry update; CMS also finalized 2025 Part D redesign changes, including the **$2,000** out-of-pocket cap, which affected MA-PD bid economics. ([cms.gov](https://www.cms.gov/newsroom/fact-sheets/2025-medicare-advantage-and-part-d-rate-announcement)) |
| **Effect on 2025 guidance / MLR expectations** | UnitedHealth’s early 2025 commentary continued to flag the “ongoing Medicare funding reductions”; Q1 2025 MCR was **84.80%**, up from **84.30%** in Q1 2024. ([prd.unitedhealthgroup.com](https://prd.unitedhealthgroup.com/content/dam/UHG/PDF/investors/2025/UNH-Reports-Q1-2025-Results-Revises-Full-Year-Guidance.pdf?utm_source=openai)) | CVS issued 2025 guidance for GAAP diluted EPS of **$4.58–$4.83**, adjusted EPS of **$5.75–$6.00**, and operating cash flow of about **$6.50 billion** after a weak 2024 HCB year; the reset implied margin repair, but from a much higher 2024 MBR base. ([investors.cvshealth.com](https://investors.cvshealth.com/news/news-details/2025/CVS-HEALTH-CORPORATION-REPORTS-FOURTH-QUARTER-AND-FULL-YEAR-2024-RESULTS)) |
| **Strategic read-through for senior acquisition** | **Scale leader with steadier growth:** UnitedHealthcare remained the largest MA platform, but 2024 growth was modest. Rate pressure lifted MCR but did not produce the same earnings collapse seen at CVS. | **Growth leader but margin-stressed:** Aetna’s 2024 MA membership growth was far faster, but it came with severe MBR deterioration, star-rating headwinds, and 2025 guidance reset risk. |

**Brief summary:** UnitedHealth was the stronger scaled incumbent for a seniors acquisition plan: larger MA base, broader top-state footprint, and only **+1.95%** MA growth, suggesting more controlled expansion. CVS/Aetna delivered much faster MA growth (**+28.53%**) but that growth coincided with a sharp deterioration in Health Care Benefits economics: **MBR rose to 92.50%** and segment adjusted operating income fell by about **$5.27 billion** year over year. CMS’s 2024 and 2025 updates were not outright nominal cuts at the all-in industry level, but the risk-model phase-in, medical-education benchmark adjustment, Star Rating effects, and Part D redesign made bid economics tighter; UnitedHealth absorbed this better, while CVS had to reset guidance and prioritize margin recovery over pure senior-membership growth.

results.question: Highlights report of American Airlines Group
results.actual_answer:
## American Airlines Group (AAL) — Highlights Report  
**As of May 8, 2026**

### Snapshot
- **Company:** American Airlines Group Inc.  
- **Ticker:** AAL  
- **Share price:** **$13.18**  
- **Market cap:** about **$8.7 billion**  
- **P/E ratio:** about **42.5x**  
- **Latest trading volume:** about **82.5 million shares**  


---

## Key Financial Highlights

### 1. Record revenue, but profitability remains pressured
American Airlines reported **record first-quarter 2026 revenue of $13.9 billion**, up **10.8% year over year**, despite an estimated **$320 million revenue impact from winter storms**. However, the company still posted a **GAAP net loss of $382 million**, or **$0.58 per diluted share**. Excluding special items, the adjusted net loss was **$267 million**, or **$0.40 per diluted share**. ([news.aa.com](https://news.aa.com/news/news-details/2026/American-Airlines-reports-first-quarter-2026-financial-results-CORP-FI-04/default.aspx))

**Takeaway:** Demand and pricing appear strong, but high fuel costs, weather disruption, and cost inflation are still limiting earnings conversion.

---

### 2. Revenue momentum improved through Q1
American said total unit revenue rose **7.6% year over year** in Q1, improving sequentially each month. March was especially strong, with both domestic and international passenger unit revenue up more than **10% year over year**. Atlantic passenger unit revenue was up **16.7%**. ([news.aa.com](https://news.aa.com/news/news-details/2026/American-Airlines-reports-first-quarter-2026-financial-results-CORP-FI-04/default.aspx))

**Takeaway:** The company is seeing stronger yield and revenue quality, especially in international and premium-heavy segments.

---

### 3. Premium, corporate, and loyalty are major growth drivers
American emphasized four commercial priorities: customer experience, global network, premium revenue, and loyalty. In Q1, **managed corporate revenue increased 13% year over year**, premium unit revenue outperformed Main Cabin, AAdvantage enrollments hit a record and were up **25%**, and co-branded credit card spend rose **9%** year over year after the expanded Citi partnership took effect. ([news.aa.com](https://news.aa.com/news/news-details/2026/American-Airlines-reports-first-quarter-2026-financial-results-CORP-FI-04/default.aspx))

**Takeaway:** The investment case is increasingly tied to higher-margin revenue streams: premium cabins, corporate travel, loyalty, and credit-card economics.

---

### 4. Balance sheet improved, but debt remains high
American ended Q1 with **total debt of $34.7 billion**, its lowest level since mid-2015, and **$10.8 billion of liquidity**. The company also reported more than **$27 billion** of unencumbered assets and first-lien borrowing capacity. ([news.aa.com](https://news.aa.com/news/news-details/2026/American-Airlines-reports-first-quarter-2026-financial-results-CORP-FI-04/default.aspx))

**Takeaway:** Debt reduction is positive, but leverage remains a central risk. American is more financially constrained than some peers, especially in a high fuel-cost or weaker demand environment.

---

## 2026 Outlook

### Guidance was reduced versus earlier expectations
At the start of 2026, American guided to **2026 adjusted EPS of $1.70 to $2.70** and free cash flow above **$2 billion**. ([news.aa.com](https://news.aa.com/news/news-details/2026/American-Airlines-reports-fourth-quarter-and-full-year-2025-financial-results-CORP-FI-01/default.aspx))  
After Q1, the company updated full-year 2026 adjusted EPS guidance to **a loss of $0.40 to profit of $1.10 per diluted share**, with the midpoint roughly flat to 2025. The company cited an expected **more than $4 billion increase in fuel expense** versus 2025. ([news.aa.com](https://news.aa.com/news/news-details/2026/American-Airlines-reports-first-quarter-2026-financial-results-CORP-FI-04/default.aspx))

**Takeaway:** The outlook deteriorated meaningfully due to fuel cost pressure. Revenue growth is strong, but much of the upside is being absorbed by higher operating costs.

---

### Q2 2026 guidance
For Q2 2026, American expects:
- **Available seat miles (ASMs):** up **4% to 6%**
- **Total revenue:** up **13.5% to 16.5%**
- **CASM excluding fuel, special items and profit sharing:** up **2% to 4%**
- **Adjusted EPS:** between **-$0.20 and +$0.20**  
([news.aa.com](https://news.aa.com/news/news-details/2026/American-Airlines-reports-first-quarter-2026-financial-results-CORP-FI-04/default.aspx))

**CASM** means **cost per available seat mile**, a key airline unit-cost metric.

---

## Investment View — Main Positives and Risks

### Positives
- Strong revenue growth and record Q1 revenue.
- Premium, corporate, and loyalty channels are outperforming.
- Debt has fallen below $35 billion for the first time since mid-2015.
- Liquidity remains solid at $10.8 billion.
- Q2 revenue growth guidance is robust.

### Risks
- Fuel cost inflation is the biggest near-term headwind.
- Profitability remains thin despite record revenue.
- High debt limits flexibility.
- Weather and operational disruptions have had material financial impacts.
- Guidance was cut sharply from the initial 2026 outlook.

---

## Bottom Line
American Airlines is showing **strong top-line momentum**, helped by premium demand, corporate travel recovery, loyalty growth, and pricing strength. However, the company remains a **high-operating-leverage, high-debt airline** where earnings are highly sensitive to fuel prices and disruptions.  

The near-term story is: **revenue momentum is improving, but fuel costs are absorbing much of the benefit.**

results.question: Which invoice is the largest invoice amount in USMF as of March 2, 2026?

results.question: I am meeting Coca-Cola and PepsiCo category leads next week. For FY2024 vs FY2023, compare beverage segment revenue (USD) and revenue mix (%) across sparkling, energy, and sports/hydration, plus consolidated gross margin. Show YoY % change and mix shift (pp) side by side.
results.actual_answer:
## Executive takeaways

- **The exact category revenue bridge requested — USD revenue and revenue mix for sparkling, energy, and sports/hydration for Coca‑Cola vs. PepsiCo — is not fully disclosed in public FY2024/FY2023 filings.**
- **Coca‑Cola** discloses beverage categories and some **unit-case volume mix**, but not net revenue by sparkling / energy / sports-hydration.
- **PepsiCo** discloses **food vs. beverage revenue mix** and division-level revenue, but not public net revenue by sparkling / energy / sports-hydration.
- The cleanest comparable public metric available for both companies is **consolidated revenue and gross margin**; for PepsiCo, a derived **total beverage revenue** estimate can be calculated from its disclosed beverage mix.

---

## Publicly disclosed / derivable FY2024 vs. FY2023 comparison

**USD in millions unless noted. Mix shift and margin change shown in percentage points (pp).**

| Company | Metric | FY2023 | FY2024 | YoY % change | Mix / margin shift |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **Coca‑Cola** | Consolidated net operating revenue | $45,754 | $47,061 | **+2.9%** | — |
| **Coca‑Cola** | Gross profit | $27,234 | $28,737 | **+5.5%** | — |
| **Coca‑Cola** | Consolidated gross margin | **59.5%** | **61.1%** | — | **+1.6 pp** |
| **Coca‑Cola** | Sparkling soft drinks — worldwide unit-case volume mix | **69%** | **69%** | n/a | **0.0 pp** |
| **PepsiCo** | Consolidated net revenue | $91,471 | $91,854 | **+0.4%** | — |
| **PepsiCo** | Beverage revenue mix of consolidated net revenue | **41%** | **42%** | — | **+1.0 pp** |
| **PepsiCo** | Derived beverage revenue | ~$37,503 | ~$38,579 | **+2.9%** | — |
| **PepsiCo** | Gross profit | $49,590 | $50,110 | **+1.0%** | — |
| **PepsiCo** | Consolidated gross margin | **54.2%** | **54.5%** | — | **+0.3 pp** |

**Sources:** Coca‑Cola FY2024 Form 10‑K reports net operating revenues of $47.061B in 2024 vs. $45.754B in 2023, gross profit of $28.737B vs. $27.234B, and gross margin improvement to 61.1% from 59.5%. It also reports sparkling soft drinks were 69% of worldwide unit-case volume in both 2024 and 2023. ([investors.coca-colacompany.com](https://investors.coca-colacompany.com/_assets/_52b069e6ac20d94ce6a3adbec798e1fb/cocacolacompany/db/1007/11020/document/2024_10-K_%28Bookmarked%29.pdf)) PepsiCo’s FY2024 annual report reports net revenue of $91.854B in 2024 vs. $91.471B in 2023, gross profit of $50.110B vs. $49.590B, and beverage mix of net revenue at 42% in 2024; its FY2023 report showed beverage mix at 41%. ([pepsico.com](https://www.pepsico.com/docs/default-source/annual-reports/2024-pepsico-annual-report.pdf?utm_source=openai))

---

## Category-level disclosure status: sparkling, energy, sports/hydration

| Company | Sparkling revenue | Energy revenue | Sports / hydration revenue | What is publicly disclosed instead |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| **Coca‑Cola** | **Not disclosed in USD** | **Not disclosed in USD** | **Not disclosed in USD** | Category groupings and volume commentary. Sparkling soft drinks were **69% of worldwide unit-case volume** in both FY2024 and FY2023. Coca‑Cola also groups brands into categories including sparkling soft drinks; water, sports, coffee and tea; juice/dairy/plant-based; and emerging beverages. |
| **PepsiCo** | **Not disclosed in USD** | **Not disclosed in USD** | **Not disclosed in USD** | Beverage vs. convenient food revenue mix; division-level results such as PepsiCo Beverages North America; brand portfolio references including Pepsi, Mountain Dew, Gatorade, Propel, Rockstar, etc. |

Coca‑Cola’s filing explicitly defines its category groupings and lists examples: sparkling soft drinks include Coca‑Cola, Sprite, Fanta and others; water, sports, coffee and tea includes BODYARMOR, Powerade, Dasani, smartwater, vitaminwater and Topo Chico; and Monster is handled through distribution relationships rather than fully owned consolidated category revenue. ([investors.coca-colacompany.com](https://investors.coca-colacompany.com/_assets/_52b069e6ac20d94ce6a3adbec798e1fb/cocacolacompany/db/1007/11020/document/2024_10-K_%28Bookmarked%29.pdf)) PepsiCo’s filings provide food/beverage mix and division-level disclosures, but not a public revenue split for carbonated soft drinks, energy, and sports/hydration. ([pepsico.com](https://www.pepsico.com/docs/default-source/annual-reports/2024-pepsico-annual-report.pdf?utm_source=openai))

---

## Readout for your category-lead meeting

1. **Coca‑Cola’s margin expansion was much stronger than PepsiCo’s in FY2024.**  
   Coca‑Cola gross margin expanded **+1.6 pp** to **61.1%**, helped by pricing, bottling refranchising, and mix; PepsiCo gross margin expanded **+0.3 pp** to **54.5%**.

2. **PepsiCo’s beverage mix increased despite flat consolidated growth.**  
   PepsiCo total revenue was essentially flat at **+0.4%**, but beverage mix rose from **41% to 42%**, implying derived beverage revenue growth of roughly **+2.9%**.

3. **Coca‑Cola remains structurally more beverage-pure and concentrate-heavy.**  
   Coca‑Cola’s economics are boosted by its concentrate model: concentrate operations were **59% of FY2024 net operating revenues** vs. **58% in FY2023**, and finished products were 41% vs. 42%. This matters because Coca‑Cola notes finished product operations generally produce higher revenue but lower gross margins than concentrate operations. ([investors.coca-colacompany.com](https://investors.coca-colacompany.com/_assets/_52b069e6ac20d94ce6a3adbec798e1fb/cocacolacompany/db/1007/11020/document/2024_10-K_%28Bookmarked%29.pdf))

4. **For sparkling specifically, Coca‑Cola’s volume mix was stable.**  
   Sparkling soft drinks stayed at **69% of Coca‑Cola worldwide unit-case volume** in both years, so the publicly disclosed volume mix shift was **0.0 pp**. That does **not** imply revenue mix was flat, because pricing, package mix, channel mix, and geography can move revenue independently of volume.

---

## Bottom line

If you need a **board-ready side-by-side by category revenue** for sparkling / energy / sports-hydration, it will require **third-party retail scanner data or syndicated market data** — e.g., Circana, NielsenIQ, Beverage Digest, Euromonitor — because the companies’ public filings do **not** disclose those category revenue splits in USD. Public filings support only the consolidated margin comparison, PepsiCo’s total beverage mix, and Coca‑Cola’s sparkling unit-volume mix.

- Terminology is mostly consistent (e.g., “gross margin,” “mix shift (pp),” “net revenue”/“net operating revenue” are applied appropriately by company). results.question: For our quarterly variance analysis, I need Tesla's GAAP operating margin percentage for the September 2025 quarter. This metric feeds into our Board presentation comparing electrical vehicle (EV) sector profitability.
results.actual_answer:
Tesla’s **GAAP operating margin for the September 2025 quarter — Q3 2025, quarter ended September 30, 2025 — was 5.8%**.

This is based on Tesla’s reported Q3 2025 financial summary:

- **Total revenues:** $28.095 billion  
- **Income from operations:** $1.624 billion  
- **Operating margin:** **5.8%**  

The calculation is:

\[
\frac{1{,}624}{28{,}095} \times 100 = 5.78\% \approx 5.8\%
\]

Tesla’s filing/financial summary explicitly lists **Operating margin = 5.8%** for Q3 2025. ([ir.tesla.com](https://ir.tesla.com/_flysystem/s3/sec/000162828026003837/tsla-20260128-gen.pdf?utm_source=openai))

results.question: For our credit evaluation of Wells Fargo (WFC), extract their long-term debt-to-equity ratio for the September 2025 quarter from financial data sources. This leverage metric is critical for assessing their capital structure risk.
results.actual_answer:
**Wells Fargo & Co. (WFC) — Long-term debt-to-equity ratio, September 2025 quarter**

**Result:** **0.97x** — equivalent to **97.1%**

**Calculation:**

| Quarter ended | Long-term debt | Total equity | Long-term debt / equity |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| **September 30, 2025** | **$177.773B** | **$183.012B** | **0.971x** |

Formula used:

\[
\text{Long-term debt-to-equity} = \frac{\text{Long-term debt}}{\text{Total equity}}
= \frac{177.773}{183.012} = 0.971
\]

**Source:** Wells Fargo’s Q3 2025 Form 10-Q reports **long-term debt of $177.773 billion** and **total equity of $183.012 billion** as of **September 30, 2025**. ([wellsfargo.com](https://www.wellsfargo.com/assets/pdf/about/investor-relations/sec-filings/2025/third-quarter-10q.pdf)) The long-term debt amount is also shown in the maturity-of-long-term-debt table. ([wellsfargo.com](https://www.wellsfargo.com/assets/pdf/about/investor-relations/sec-filings/2025/third-quarter-10q.pdf))

**Credit read-through:** A ~**0.97x** long-term debt-to-equity ratio indicates that Wells Fargo had slightly less long-term debt than total book equity at quarter-end. For a bank, this should be interpreted alongside regulatory capital and liquidity metrics—WFC also reported a **6.42% supplementary leverage ratio** and **7.71% Tier 1 leverage ratio** for the same quarter. ([wellsfargo.com](https://www.wellsfargo.com/assets/pdf/about/investor-relations/sec-filings/2025/third-quarter-10q.pdf))

tag_reasoning.clarity:
- Acronyms/jargon: Most terms are standard for the intended audience (WFC, 10-Q, debt-to-equity). However, “supplementary leverage ratio (SLR)” and “Tier 1 leverage ratio” are introduced without defining SLR, and “credit read-through” is somewhat context-specific. - Consistent terminology: The response consistently uses “long-term debt-to-equity ratio” / “long-term debt / equity” and applies the same labels across the table, formula, and narrative without switching concepts.
tag_reasoning.relevance:

It adds some credit context (SLR/Tier 1 leverage) that is slightly tangential but still related to leverage/capital structure, so only a small relevance deduction.
- It provides a clear numeric ratio, the component values (long-term debt and total equity), the calculation, quarter-end date, and a cited source, which is sufficient and not vague.
tag_reasoning.structure:

- It is clearly organized with a title, result section, calculation table, formula, sources, and a brief credit interpretation, using headings and formatting effectively.
tag_reasoning.citations: The response clearly cites the source as Wells Fargo’s Q3 2025 Form 10‑Q and provides direct, traceable external URLs (wellsfargo.com PDF links) to the filing used for the long-term debt and total equity figures.

results.question: For a credit limit review, I need S&P Global's book value per share (BVPS) for FY2022 through FY2024, including year-over-year percentage changes and the three-year CAGR. How does that BVPS trend compare with Moody's over the same period to help gauge the equity buffer?
results.actual_answer:
**Assumption:** I’m using fiscal year-end BVPS (book value per share) at **Dec. 31** for each company. CAGR is calculated from **FY2022 to FY2024**, which is a two-year compounding interval across three fiscal year-end observations.

### BVPS trend: S&P Global vs. Moody’s

| Company | FY2022 BVPS | FY2023 BVPS | YoY change | FY2024 BVPS | YoY change | FY2022–FY2024 CAGR |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **S&P Global (SPGI)** | **$110.87** | **$106.52** | **-3.9%** | **$105.57** | **-0.9%** | **-2.4%** |
| **Moody’s (MCO)** | **$14.68** | **$19.05** | **+29.8%** | **$20.67** | **+8.5%** | **+18.7%** |

S&P Global’s FY2024 BVPS of **$105.57** is also reported by AlphaQuery, which defines BVPS as **total shareholders’ equity divided by common shares outstanding**; Macrotrends’ year-end price-to-book table shows the same FY2024 year-end BVPS and the FY2022–FY2023 comparatives. ([alphaquery.com](https://www.alphaquery.com/stock/SPGI/fundamentals/annual/book-value-per-share)) Moody’s FY2024 year-end BVPS of **$20.67**, with FY2023 at **$19.05** and FY2022 at **$14.68**, is shown in Macrotrends’ price-to-book history. ([macrotrends.net](https://macrotrends.net/stocks/charts/MCO/moodys/price-book?utm_source=openai))

### Credit-limit interpretation / equity-buffer read-through

- **S&P Global:** BVPS was **slightly negative over the period**, declining from **$110.87 to $105.57**. The decline was modest, but it indicates that the per-share GAAP equity buffer did **not expand** from FY2022 to FY2024. For credit-limit purposes, this is not necessarily alarming given S&P Global’s scale and profitability, but the book-equity cushion per share was broadly flat-to-down.

- **Moody’s:** BVPS rose sharply, from **$14.68 to $20.67**, producing an **~18.7% CAGR**. That suggests Moody’s expanded its accounting equity base per share materially over the same window.

- **Relative comparison:** S&P Global still has a **much larger absolute BVPS** than Moody’s — roughly **5.1x Moody’s FY2024 BVPS** — but Moody’s showed the **stronger trend**. In credit terms, S&P Global appears to have the **larger absolute book-equity cushion per share**, while Moody’s demonstrated **faster equity-buffer accretion** from FY2022–FY2024.

**Bottom line:** For a credit limit review, S&P Global’s BVPS trend is stable but slightly eroding; Moody’s trend is clearly positive. If the focus is absolute book-equity depth, S&P Global screens stronger. If the focus is momentum in equity-buffer build, Moody’s compares more favorably.

tag_reasoning.clarity:
- Acronyms/jargon: BVPS is expanded and briefly defined (equity ÷ shares). Other acronyms used (FY, YoY, CAGR, GAAP) are standard for the target audience, so no issue. Minor clarity gap: “price-to-book table” and “year-end” references could be misconstrued as market-derived rather than accounting BVPS unless explicitly tied to reported equity/share counts.
- Consistent terminology: Generally consistent use of BVPS and “book-equity/equity buffer.” Small inconsistency in phrasing (“book-equity cushion per share,” “GAAP equity buffer,” “equity-buffer accretion”) that refers to the same idea but with multiple labels, slightly reducing terminological consistency.
tag_reasoning.relevance: The response is tightly aligned with the request: it provides BVPS for FY2022–FY2024 for S&P Global, includes YoY % changes and a FY2022–FY2024 CAGR, and explicitly compares the trend versus Moody’s with an equity-buffer interpretation. Minor relevance gaps: it adds sourcing/methodology detail and a brief credit-limit interpretation that’s adjacent to (but still supportive of) the “gauge the equity buffer” goal; and it assumes Dec. 31 fiscal year-ends without confirming fiscal-year convention, which could matter for “FY” framing. Overall, the information is specific and sufficient rather than vague, and no “missing data” limitation needed to be stated because the response supplies figures for both companies.

results.question: I'm preparing a comparison presentation about Rigetti's advertising competitors. Compare Rigetti's FY2024 ROE against D-Wave Quantum and IONQ. Include FY2022-2024 EPS in USD, show YoY EPS growth each year, and add P/E. I want to see if Rigetti's earnings momentum lines up with where it's trading. Provide me with brief explanation of the earning momentum.
results.actual_answer:
## Rigetti vs. D-Wave Quantum vs. IonQ — FY2024 ROE, EPS momentum, and P/E

**Assumptions:**  
- **ROE** = return on equity, using FY2024 annual ratios.  
- **EPS** = basic earnings per share in USD.  
- **YoY EPS growth** is calculated as change vs. prior year using `change / |prior-year EPS|`, so for loss-making companies a positive number means **loss per share narrowed**, while a negative number means **loss per share worsened**.  
- **P/E** shown is the **current P/E as of May 7, 2026 close** where available; for loss-making trailing earnings it is **not meaningful (N.M.)**. StockAnalysis shows RGTI at **$18.34**, QBTS at **$21.99**, and IONQ at **$47.68** as of the May 7, 2026 close. ([stockanalysis.com](https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/rgti/financials/ratios/))

| Company | Ticker | FY2024 ROE | FY2022 EPS | FY2022 YoY EPS growth | FY2023 EPS | FY2023 YoY EPS growth | FY2024 EPS | FY2024 YoY EPS growth | Current P/E |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **Rigetti Computing** | RGTI | **-170.20%** | **-$0.70** | **+57.3%** | **-$0.57** | **+18.6%** | **-$1.09** | **-91.2%** | **N.M.** |
| **D-Wave Quantum** | QBTS | **-763.09%** | **-$0.45** | **-36.4%** | **-$0.60** | **-33.3%** | **-$0.75** | **-25.0%** | **N.M.** |
| **IonQ** | IONQ | **-76.34%** | **-$0.25** | **+67.5%** | **-$0.78** | **-212.0%** | **-$1.56** | **-100.0%** | **45.59x** |

Rigetti reported EPS of **-$0.70 in FY2022**, **-$0.57 in FY2023**, and **-$1.09 in FY2024**; its FY2024 ROE was **-170.20%**, and StockAnalysis’ current P/E field is not meaningful because trailing earnings are negative. ([stockanalysis.com](https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/rgti/financials/)) D-Wave’s EPS moved from **-$0.45** to **-$0.60** to **-$0.75** from FY2022–FY2024, with FY2024 ROE of **-763.09%** and no meaningful current P/E due to losses. ([stockanalysis.com](https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/qbts/financials/)) IonQ’s EPS moved from **-$0.25** to **-$0.78** to **-$1.56**, with FY2024 ROE of **-76.34%**; its current P/E is shown as **45.59x**, reflecting positive current/TTM earnings in the ratios table, not positive FY2024 earnings. ([stockanalysis.com](https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/ionq/financials/))

### Brief earnings-momentum read-through

**Rigetti’s earnings momentum does not clearly line up with its trading level on an earnings basis.** Its EPS improved in FY2023, with the loss per share narrowing from **-$0.70 to -$0.57**, but that momentum reversed sharply in FY2024 as EPS fell to **-$1.09**, a roughly **91% worsening** in loss per share. Its FY2024 ROE of **-170.20%** also shows that losses were still very large relative to book equity.

**Relative to peers:**  
- **Rigetti looks better than D-Wave on EPS deterioration**, because D-Wave’s losses widened every year from FY2022 to FY2024.  
- **Rigetti looks worse than IonQ on FY2024 ROE**, because IonQ’s FY2024 ROE was less negative at **-76.34%** versus Rigetti’s **-170.20%**.  
- **None of the three had positive FY2024 EPS**, so traditional P/E is a weak valuation anchor for this group. IonQ’s current positive P/E appears to be driven by more recent trailing results/non-operating effects rather than FY2024 profitability.

**Bottom line for your presentation:** Rigetti’s stock appears to be trading more on quantum-computing optionality, balance-sheet runway, technical milestones, and sector sentiment than on realized earnings momentum. FY2024 EPS momentum was negative, ROE was deeply negative, and current P/E is not meaningful—so the earnings profile does **not** yet support the valuation in a conventional earnings-multiple framework.

results.question: To support equity research and portfolio allocation decisions, evaluate the competitive positioning of Morgan Stanley, BlackRock, and Charles Schwab within U.S. financial services. Present a side-by-side table comparing FY2023 and FY2024 EBITDA, EBITDA margin, and year-over-year EBITDA growth, and briefly identify which firm shows the strongest EBITDA momentum and why.
results.actual_answer:
**Assumptions / caveat:** EBITDA is less commonly used for banks and broker-dealers than pre-tax income, return on tangible common equity, net interest margin, or operating margin. To keep the comparison consistent, I use **reported/trailing annual EBITDA and revenue-based EBITDA margins from Macrotrends** for FY2023 and FY2024, with margins calculated as EBITDA ÷ revenue where needed. Figures are approximate and in **US$ billions**.

| Company | Competitive position in U.S. financial services | FY2023 EBITDA | FY2023 EBITDA margin | FY2024 EBITDA | FY2024 EBITDA margin | YoY EBITDA growth |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **Morgan Stanley (MS)** | Leading wealth-management and institutional-securities franchise; benefits from diversified fee-based wealth assets plus cyclical upside from investment banking and trading. 2024 results were supported by stronger markets and improving investment banking; firm-wide wealth and investment-management client assets reached about **$7.9T**. ([morganstanley.com](https://www.morganstanley.com/about-us-ir/shareholder/4q2024.pdf?utm_source=openai)) | **$16.1B** | **29.7%** | **$22.8B** | **36.8%** | **+41.6%** ([macrotrends.net](https://www.macrotrends.net/stocks/charts/MS/morgan-stanley/ebitda?utm_source=openai)) |
| **BlackRock (BLK)** | Dominant global asset manager with scale advantages in ETFs/iShares, institutional mandates, private markets expansion, and Aladdin technology. FY2024 revenue rose **14%**, crossing **$20B**, helped by markets, organic base-fee growth, performance fees, technology revenue, and GIP acquisition contribution. ([ir.blackrock.com](https://ir.blackrock.com/news-and-events/press-releases/press-releases-details/2025/BlackRock-Reports-Full-Year-2024-Diluted-EPS-of-42.01-or-43.61-as-Adjusted-Fourth-Quarter-2024-Diluted-EPS-of-10.63-or-11.93-as-Adjusted/default.aspx?utm_source=openai)) | **$6.7B** | **37.5%** | **$8.2B** | **40.0%** | **+21.7%** ([macrotrends.net](https://www.macrotrends.net/stocks/charts/BLK/blackrock/ebitda-margin?utm_source=openai)) |
| **Charles Schwab (SCHW)** | Scale retail brokerage / wealth platform with large client-asset base, strong advisory and asset-management economics, and banking spread exposure. FY2024 total client assets reached about **$10.1T**, up from **$8.5T** in 2023; results improved as trading, asset-management fees, and client engagement strengthened, though net interest revenue remained a sensitivity. ([content.schwab.com](https://content.schwab.com/web/retail/public/about-schwab/schwab_annual_report_2024.pdf?utm_source=openai)) | **$10.0B** | **53.2%** | **$11.3B** | **57.5%** | **+12.5%** ([macrotrends.net](https://www.macrotrends.net/stocks/charts/SCHW/charles-schwab/ebitda?utm_source=openai)) |

### Strongest EBITDA momentum

**Morgan Stanley shows the strongest EBITDA momentum**, with FY2024 EBITDA up about **41.6% year over year**, far ahead of BlackRock’s **21.7%** and Schwab’s **12.5%**. The key driver appears to be **operating leverage from a cyclical rebound**: Morgan Stanley benefited from stronger capital-markets activity, improved investment banking, and market-sensitive wealth/investment-management revenues, which expanded its EBITDA margin from roughly **29.7% to 36.8%**.

From an allocation perspective:

- **Morgan Stanley**: strongest near-term EBITDA acceleration, but more cyclical exposure to capital markets.
- **BlackRock**: best structural scale story; margin expansion supported by AUM growth, ETFs, technology, and private markets.
- **Schwab**: highest EBITDA margin in this peer set, but EBITDA growth was more modest and remains sensitive to client cash, rates, and net interest revenue dynamics.

results.question: I'm scoping 2025 brand partnerships and need to assess which companies have the strongest earnings base to support aggressive product innovation pipelines. Show Unilever yearly EPS in USD for 2023 and 2024, side by side with Procter & Gamble and Nestle. Include YoY % per year and a two year trend table. Crosscheck with two to three sources and give a one line read on who has the strongest EPS momentum backing their product development capacity.
results.actual_answer:
**Assumptions:** using **reported EPS (Earnings per Share)**, not adjusted/core EPS; USD figures are **company-reported USD where available** and **Unilever converted from EUR to USD** using annual average EUR/USD rates. P&G fiscal years end **June 30**; Unilever and Nestlé fiscal years end **December 31**, so comparability is close but not perfectly calendar-aligned.

### Yearly EPS in USD — 2023 vs. 2024

| Company | FY2023 EPS, USD | FY2023 YoY | FY2024 EPS, USD | FY2024 YoY | 2-year direction |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|
| **Unilever** | **$2.79** | **~‑11.7%** | **$2.49** | **~‑10.9%** | Down both years |
| **Procter & Gamble** | **$5.90** | **+1.5%** | **$6.02** | **+2.0%** | Steady positive |
| **Nestlé** | **$4.72** | **+31.8%** | **$4.76** | **+0.8%** | Big 2023 rebound, flat 2024 |

### Two-year trend table

| Rank by EPS momentum | Company | 2023 → 2024 EPS change | Read-through for innovation funding capacity |
|---:|---|---:|---|
| **1** | **Procter & Gamble** | **+$0.12 / +2.0%** | Best *ongoing* EPS momentum: modest but positive, with the highest absolute EPS in both years. |
| **2** | **Nestlé** | **+$0.04 / +0.8%** | Strong earnings base, but 2024 EPS momentum was nearly flat after a large 2023 rebound. |
| **3** | **Unilever** | **‑$0.30 / ‑10.9%** | EPS declined in 2024, though underlying EPS improved; reported EPS is less supportive on a headline basis. |

**Source crosscheck:** Unilever’s official FY2024 release reports basic EPS of **€2.30 in 2024 vs. €2.58 in 2023**, and diluted EPS of **€2.29 vs. €2.56**; it also shows underlying diluted EPS rising to **€2.98 from €2.60**, which explains why reported EPS looks weaker than operating momentum. ([unilever.com](https://www.unilever.com/files/92ui5egz/production/e6b5cde434e49e67b8aa59810ba1fa379ecc12ad.pdf)) P&G’s annual report shows diluted net EPS of **$6.02 in FY2024 vs. $5.90 in FY2023**, while StockAnalysis independently lists the same FY2024/FY2023 periods and USD financials. ([us.pg.com](https://us.pg.com/annualreport2024/financial-highlights/)) Nestlé’s FY2024 release reports basic EPS of **CHF 4.19 vs. CHF 4.24** and underlying EPS of **CHF 4.77 vs. CHF 4.80**; Nestlé’s Annual Review provides the illustrative USD EPS crosscheck of **$4.76 in 2024 vs. $4.72 in 2023**, and its 2023 Annual Review shows **$4.72 in 2023 vs. $3.58 in 2022**. ([nestle.com](https://www.nestle.com/media/pressreleases/allpressreleases/full-year-results-2024)) For Unilever’s EUR-to-USD conversion, I used annual average EUR/USD rates of about **1.0813 for 2023** and **1.0822 for 2024**. ([bundesbank.de](https://www.bundesbank.de/resource/blob/927760/3d95a8347dbd5b141341266d38d3dd15/472B63F073F071307366337C94F8C870/2024-03-15-11-36-18-wechselkursstatistik-data.pdf?utm_source=openai))

**One-line read:** **P&G has the strongest EPS momentum backing product-development capacity**—not the fastest historical rebound, but the most consistent positive EPS progression and the highest absolute EPS base across 2023–2024.

results.question: I'm benchmarking counterparty liquidity across our two largest non-financial exposures. Compare Coca-Cola and Johnson & Johnson's quick ratio and current ratio from FY2021 through FY2024 in a table, and calculate the percentage change over that period for each metric. Based on these trends, flag which counterparty shows weaker liquidity trajectory heading into upcoming refinancing cycles.
results.actual_answer:
**Assumption / method:** I used fiscal year-end consolidated balance sheet data and calculated:  
- **Current ratio** = current assets ÷ current liabilities  
- **Quick ratio** = (current assets − inventories) ÷ current liabilities  
Dollar data are in USD millions; ratios are shown as “x.” Coca-Cola’s fiscal year ends December 31; Johnson & Johnson’s FY2024 ended December 29, 2024. Source data are from company SEC filings / annual reports. ([sec.gov](https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/21344/000002134425000011/ko-20241231.htm))

| Counterparty | Metric | FY2021 | FY2022 | FY2023 | FY2024 | % change FY2021→FY2024 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **Coca-Cola** | **Quick ratio** | 0.96x | 0.93x | 0.95x | 0.84x | **-12.2%** |
| **Coca-Cola** | **Current ratio** | 1.13x | 1.15x | 1.13x | 1.03x | **-8.9%** |
| **Johnson & Johnson** | **Quick ratio** | 1.12x | 0.77x | 0.91x | 0.86x | **-22.8%** |
| **Johnson & Johnson** | **Current ratio** | 1.35x | 0.99x | 1.16x | 1.11x | **-17.6%** |

### Liquidity trajectory read-through

**Johnson & Johnson shows the weaker liquidity trajectory** over FY2021–FY2024. Its **quick ratio fell 22.8%** and **current ratio fell 17.6%**, a materially larger deterioration than Coca-Cola’s **12.2%** and **8.9%** declines, respectively. J&J also dipped below 1.0x on both quick and current ratios in FY2022, indicating a period when current liabilities exceeded current assets and liquid current assets by these measures. ([sec.gov](https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/200406/000119312523072650/d398151dars.pdf?utm_source=openai))

That said, **Coca-Cola’s FY2024 position is tighter on an absolute quick-ratio basis**: 0.84x vs. J&J’s 0.86x, and its current ratio is closer to 1.0x at 1.03x vs. J&J’s 1.11x. Coca-Cola’s FY2024 current liabilities rose to $25.249 billion while current assets declined to $25.997 billion, compressing both metrics. ([sec.gov](https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/21344/000002134425000011/ko-20241231.htm))

**Flag for refinancing cycles:**  
- **Trend risk:** **Johnson & Johnson** — sharper multi-year deterioration from a stronger FY2021 base.  
- **Point-in-time liquidity tightness:** **Coca-Cola** — lower FY2024 current ratio and essentially comparable but slightly weaker quick-ratio cushion.

**Overall counterparty watchlist flag:** **Johnson & Johnson for weaker liquidity trajectory**, with Coca-Cola also worth monitoring because its FY2024 liquidity cushion is thin and close to 1.0x.

results.question: Preparing for a client briefing with Intuit: What was Intuit's Return on Equity for fiscal year 2024, how did it change versus fiscal year 2023, and how does FY2024 ROE compare with Adobe and Salesforce? Please also summarize the three year ROE trend from FY2022 through FY2024.
results.actual_answer:
## ROE snapshot — Intuit FY2024 vs. FY2023 and peers

**Method used:** Return on Equity (ROE) = **GAAP net income ÷ average shareholders’ equity** for the fiscal year. Average equity = average of beginning- and end-of-year shareholders’ equity. Figures are in USD millions.

### Key takeaways

- **Intuit FY2024 ROE was ~16.6%**, up from **~14.1% in FY2023** — an increase of about **+2.5 percentage points**.
- The improvement was driven primarily by **higher net income**: Intuit’s FY2024 net income rose to **$2.963B**, from **$2.384B** in FY2023. ([fintel.io](https://fintel.io/doc/sec-intuit-inc-896878-10k-2024-september-04-19970-8967?utm_source=openai))
- Versus peers in FY2024, Intuit’s ROE was:
  - **Well below Adobe** at **~36.3%**
  - **Well above Salesforce** at **~7.0%**
- Over FY2022–FY2024, Intuit’s ROE was relatively stable but improved in FY2024: **15.7% → 14.1% → 16.6%**.

## ROE calculation table

| Company | Fiscal year | Net income | Avg. shareholders’ equity | ROE |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **Intuit** | FY2022 | $2,066 | $13,155 | **15.7%** |
| **Intuit** | FY2023 | $2,384 | $16,855 | **14.1%** |
| **Intuit** | FY2024 | $2,963 | $17,853 | **16.6%** |
| **Adobe** | FY2022 | $4,756 | $14,424 | **33.0%** |
| **Adobe** | FY2023 | $5,428 | $15,285 | **35.5%** |
| **Adobe** | FY2024 | $5,560 | $15,312 | **36.3%** |
| **Salesforce** | FY2022 | $1,444 | $49,842 | **2.9%** |
| **Salesforce** | FY2023 | $208 | $58,245 | **0.4%** |
| **Salesforce** | FY2024 | $4,136 | $59,003 | **7.0%** |

Intuit shareholders’ equity was **$16.441B in FY2022, $17.269B in FY2023, and $18.436B in FY2024**; FY2021 equity was **$9.869B**, used as the beginning equity base for FY2022. ([stockanalysis.com](https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/intu/financials/balance-sheet/?utm_source=openai)) Adobe’s net income was **$4.756B, $5.428B, and $5.560B** for FY2022–FY2024, while shareholders’ equity was **$14.051B, $16.518B, and $14.105B** for FY2022–FY2024. ([stockanalysis.com](https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/adbe/financials/?utm_source=openai)) Salesforce’s net income was **$1.444B, $208M, and $4.136B** for FY2022–FY2024, and shareholders’ equity was **$58.131B, $58.359B, and $59.646B** for FY2022–FY2024. ([stockanalysis.com](https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/crm/financials/?utm_source=openai))

## Three-year trend: FY2022–FY2024

**Intuit:** ROE dipped from **15.7% in FY2022** to **14.1% in FY2023**, then rebounded to **16.6% in FY2024**. The FY2024 improvement reflects stronger profitability outpacing growth in the equity base.

**Adobe:** ROE increased steadily from **33.0% to 35.5% to 36.3%**. Adobe’s ROE is structurally higher than Intuit’s in this period, helped by high margins and a lower equity base, partly influenced by ongoing share repurchases.

**Salesforce:** ROE was much lower and more volatile: **2.9% → 0.4% → 7.0%**. FY2024 marked a major rebound as net income rose sharply from FY2023’s depressed level. Salesforce FY2024 net income increased to **$4.136B**, from **$208M** in FY2023. ([macrotrends.net](https://www.macrotrends.net/stocks/charts/CRM/salesforce/net-income?utm_source=openai))

## Client-briefing sound bite

**Intuit generated a solid FY2024 ROE of approximately 16.6%, improving by about 250 basis points versus FY2023. That places Intuit comfortably ahead of Salesforce’s FY2024 ROE of roughly 7.0%, but meaningfully behind Adobe’s roughly 36.3%, which reflects Adobe’s higher profitability relative to its equity base. Over the last three fiscal years, Intuit’s ROE has been broadly stable in the mid-teens, with a FY2024 rebound after a FY2023 dip.**

results.question: We're evaluating a manufacturing joint venture with Caterpillar and need their current ratio for September 2025 to verify stable liquidity for long-term production commitments.
results.actual_answer:
**Caterpillar Inc. current ratio at September 30, 2025: ~1.38x**

**Calculation**

\[
\text{Current ratio} = \frac{\text{Total current assets}}{\text{Total current liabilities}}
\]

\[
= \frac{49{,}818\text{ million}}{35{,}991\text{ million}} = 1.384 \approx 1.38x
\]

Caterpillar reported **total current assets of $49.818 billion** and **total current liabilities of $35.991 billion** as of **September 30, 2025** in its Q3 2025 financial statements. ([sec.gov](https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/18230/000001823025000048/cat-20250930.htm?utm_source=openai))

**Liquidity read-through for a manufacturing JV**

A **1.38x current ratio** indicates Caterpillar had about **$1.38 of current assets for every $1.00 of current liabilities** at quarter-end. That generally supports a view of **stable near-term liquidity**, especially for a large industrial manufacturer with recurring inventory, receivables, and financing operations. However, for long-term production commitments, I’d treat this as a **positive but not standalone** indicator—also review operating cash flow, debt maturities, inventory quality, receivables collectability, and any JV-specific capital contribution obligations.

results.question: Building comps for a potential logistics bolt on. How do UPS and FedEx stack up on FY2024 revenue growth year over year, operating margin trend from FY2022 to FY2024, and FY2024 EV to EBITDA? present me these information on a comparison table. I want a read on trajectory and valuation before outreach. Also, please prepare a small summary/analysis of what the results show.
results.actual_answer:
**Assumptions / basis:** UPS FY2024 is the year ended **Dec. 31, 2024**; FedEx FY2024 is the year ended **May 31, 2024**. Revenue and operating margin are based on reported GAAP revenue and operating income. EV/EBITDA is shown on a **FY2024 fiscal-year basis** using enterprise value and EBITDA from StockAnalysis ratio data where available; figures are rounded.

| Company | FY2024 revenue | FY2024 revenue growth YoY | Operating margin FY2022 | Operating margin FY2023 | Operating margin FY2024 | Operating margin trend FY2022 → FY2024 | FY2024 EV / EBITDA | Read-through |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|---:|---|
| **UPS** | **$91.3B** | **+0.3%** | **11.0%** | **5.5%** | **6.1%** | Sharp reset from 2022 peak; modest rebound in 2024, but still materially below 2022 | **~10.3x** | Slight top-line stabilization, but profitability remains well below pandemic/post-pandemic peak levels |
| **FedEx** | **$87.7B** | **-2.7%** | **6.7%** | **5.4%** | **6.3%** | Down in 2023, then stronger recovery in 2024; nearly back to FY2022 margin | **~9.3x** | Revenue still contracting, but cost actions / network transformation appear to be supporting margin recovery |

**Source notes:** UPS reported FY2024 revenue of about $91.3B and 2024 operating profit of about $5.6B, versus $91.0B revenue and $5.0B operating profit in 2023 and $100.3B revenue and $11.1B operating profit in 2022. ([about.ups.com](https://about.ups.com/ae/en/newsroom/press-releases/financials/ups-releases-4q-2024-earnings-and-provides-2025-guidance.html?utm_source=openai)) FedEx reported FY2024 revenue of $87.7B, down 2.7% from FY2023; FedEx’s FY2023 annual report shows FY2023 revenue of $90.2B, FY2022 revenue of $93.5B, FY2023 operating income of $4.9B and FY2022 operating income of $6.2B. ([newsroom.fedex.com](https://newsroom.fedex.com/fedex-reports-higher-full-year-diluted-eps-of-17-21-and-adjusted-diluted-eps-of-17-80?utm_source=openai)) FY2024 EV/EBITDA is based on StockAnalysis ratio data: FedEx FY2024 EV/EBITDA of 9.26x and UPS FY2024 enterprise value of $124.7B; UPS FY2024 EBITDA of about $12.1B implies ~10.3x. ([stockanalysis.com](https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/fdx/financials/ratios/?utm_source=openai))

### Quick analysis

- **Growth trajectory:** Neither name is showing strong revenue momentum in FY2024. **UPS is essentially flat** at +0.3%, while **FedEx is still shrinking** at -2.7%. For a logistics bolt-on, that suggests the large-cap parcel backdrop remains muted rather than expansionary.
- **Margin trajectory:** **FedEx looks better on margin recovery**: FY2024 operating margin improved to ~6.3%, close to FY2022’s ~6.7%. **UPS improved only modestly** from FY2023’s trough and remains far below FY2022’s ~11.0%, suggesting more earnings normalization still needed.
- **Valuation:** On FY2024 EV/EBITDA, **FedEx screens slightly cheaper** at ~9.3x versus **UPS at ~10.3x**. That premium for UPS is notable because its margin recovery appears less advanced.
- **Outreach implication:** If using these as public comps for a logistics bolt-on, I’d frame the sector as **low-growth but operationally levered**. A target with above-market organic growth, stable customer mix, or structurally higher margins could justify a premium; otherwise, the public-comp anchor likely sits around a **high-single-digit to low-double-digit EV/EBITDA range**, with FedEx implying the lower end and UPS the higher end.
